_meta.notes: Chapter 1 Lesson 8. Previously taught: kumusta, mabuti, ikaw, magandang, umaga, gabi, po, naman, sige, oo, hindi, opo, salamat, walang anuman, pasensya na.

cards.body: So far you've learned to be polite. Now let's talk like friends. No po needed here.
Siyempre means 'of course!' Use it when the answer is obvious.

example.question: Are you coming to the party?
example.note: Enthusiastic and casual. Only with friends.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: Siyempre comes from the Spanish 'siempre' (always). Filipinos turned it into 'of course!'

cards.body: Pare means 'bro' or 'dude.' It's what you call a close male friend.

example.note: Casual and friendly. Never use pare with elders.

cards.source_text: Your friend Jun asks: Want to grab food?
cards.prompt: Obviously yes. What do you say?
cards.explanation: Siyempre, pare! = Of course, bro! Casual, friendly, no po.

cards.body: Mare is the same as pare but for a close female friend.

example.note: Pare = male friend. Mare = female friend.

cards.source_text: Your friend Maria asks: Did you like the food?
cards.prompt: It was amazing. What do you say?
cards.explanation: Siyempre, mare! = Of course, sis! Casual with a close female friend.

cards.body: With friends: siyempre, pare, mare, no po. With elders and authority: po, opo, sige po. Mixing them up sounds either too stiff or disrespectful.

cards.source_text: Two people ask if you're coming. Your friend Jun and your boss.
cards.prompt: Which pair of replies is correct?
options: To Jun: Siyempre, pare! / To boss: Opo!
To Jun: Opo! / To boss: Siyempre, pare!
To Jun: Sige po! / To boss: Sige, pare!
cards.explanation: Casual with friends (siyempre, pare), respectful with authority (opo).

cards.source_text: Your friend Maria invites you to karaoke. Obviously you're going.
cards.explanation: Siyempre = of course! Add mare for extra friendliness.

wrong_answer_tracking.behaviour: Wrong answers go to back of current session queue. User must get ALL questions right to complete. Cannot finish lesson with outstanding wrong answers.
wrong_answer_tracking.wrong_count_usage: Feed to Gemini system prompt for AI Practice.

alam_mo_ba: Pare comes from the Spanish 'compadre' and mare from 'comadre.' Originally godparent terms, now they just mean close friend.
Using po with a close friend your age can actually sound sarcastic. Context matters.
Some friend groups use 'pre' (shortened pare) or 'mars' (shortened mare) in text messages.
